title: Mandevilla hesperia
taxonomy: Mandevilla hesperia is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae, commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. This plant is part of the genus Mandevilla, which includes around 30 species of flowering plants. Mandevilla hesperia is native to the tropical regions of South America, specifically in Argentina, Brazil, and Paraguay. It is a climber or vine that thrives in warm and humid environments. The plant is known for its showy, trumpet-shaped flowers that come in a range of colors, including pink, white, and purple.

side_effects: Mandevilla hesperia is known to be toxic to humans and animals if ingested, and handling the plant can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. The sap of the plant may cause redness, itching, and blistering on the skin if it comes into contact with it. intro: Mandevilla hesperia, also known as the white Mandevilla or Mandevilla, is a flowering vine native to South America, characterized by its white, funnel-shaped flowers and heart-shaped leaves. The plant has been traditionally used in medicine due to its potential health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which can aid in reducing stress and promoting overall well-being. The bioactive constituents of Mandevilla hesperia include alkaloids, flavonoids, and glycosides, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Mandevilla hesperia is often used to make herbal teas, infusions, and extracts, which are commonly consumed to promote relaxation and alleviate symptoms of anxiety and insomnia.
